visits.text: Moskenes→Bodø ferry: absolutely must book in advance in summer - without reservation, wait time can exceed 6h at the Moskenes dock. The crossing lasts 3h15 to 3h45 across Vestfjord, sometimes choppy. It's also on this route that you can spot eagles and, in season, whales.

From Bodø, head to Saltstraumen 30 km southeast. In this 150-meter-wide strait, 400 million cubic meters of water rush through every 6 hours when the tide reverses, reaching 22 knots (40 km/h) and forming whirlpools 10 meters in diameter and 5 meters deep. It's officially the world's strongest tidal current, a phenomenon 2,000 to 3,000 years old. The spectacle is most intense two to three days before or after the full and new moon - check tide times before going.

activities.text: If you have energy, climb Mount Keiservarden from Bodø for a panoramic view over the city, the sea and the silhouettes of the Lofoten on the horizon. It's also the evening in Bodø that you organize what's next: the flight to Tromsø the following morning.

days_plan.itin_title: Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: Henningsvær, nicknamed the 'Venice of Lofoten', spreads across several small islands connected by bridges - from Google Maps, you can clearly see the shape of the miniature archipelago. Park at the entrance and explore on foot: the streets are too narrow for cars. The harbor and its old converted fishing warehouses are classified as Kulturminne (Norwegian cultural heritage). Cod fishing remains the main activity - in February-March, the cod season transforms the quays into a scene of continuous work.

Henningsvær's football field is probably the most photographed in the world: built on a rocky islet between two arms of sea, without stands - spectators bring their own chairs. The local club uses it for amateur matches, and the site also hosts concerts and festivals.

The KaviarFactory is one of the most unexpected cultural surprises in Northern Norway. Opened in 2013 in a former 1950s caviar factory rehabilitated into 500 m² of exhibition space, it presents internationally renowned artists - Ai Weiwei, Marina Abramovic, Yoko Ono have all exhibited here. Next door, Galleri Lofoten displays the largest collection of painting from the 'golden age of North Norwegian art' (1870-1930). Two world-class museums in a village of 500 inhabitants.

activities.text: For a short but striking hike, climb Festvågtind (541 m). The ascent is steep and short, but the 360° view over Henningsvær, the archipelago and the open sea is one of the best in the eastern Lofoten. Allow 2h30 round trip. Operators also offer sea kayaking in the islets around the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: The Abisko-Narvik road (85 km, 1h15) crosses the Norwegian border at Riksgränsen and descends toward the fjords. In Narvik, begin at the War Museum (Narviksenteret). The Battle of Narvik from April 9 to June 8, 1940 lasted 62 days and cost 8,500 lives. It was at Narvik that Allied forces - British, French, Polish and Norwegian - won their very first land victory against Nazi Germany. The museum recounts these 62 days battle by battle, through photos, films and audio testimonies. Allow 1.5 hours.

Narvik's harbor deserves a stroll: it's why the battle took place. Narvik was the export port for iron ore from Kiruna (Sweden) - an ice-free port year-round, connected to the Swedish mines by the Ofotbanen, the railway line built in 1902. Hitler needed this ore for his war industry: 90% of Swedish iron passed through Narvik in winter. The city still lives to the rhythm of this rail traffic.

activities.text: Take the cable car up Narvikfjellet for a panoramic view of the city, Ofotfjord and mountains from 656 meters. In summer under the midnight sun, the panorama is striking; in winter, it's one of the best vantage points for northern lights away from city lights.

days_plan.itin_title: Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: Reine is often described as Norway's most beautiful village - hard to dispute when you arrive by road and the red cabins on stilts suddenly appear facing the peaks plunging into Reinefjord. Reine's rorbuer, painted red, were once the lodgings of fishermen who came from the mainland to work the cod from January to April. Many have been converted into accommodations - book well in advance.

A few kilometers away, the village of Å (pronounced 'Aw') is the last in the archipelago - the road ends there. This end of the world is visited like an open-air museum: a boathouse, a cod liver oil factory, a forge, an old post office, rorbuer. The Norwegian Fishing Village Museum traces the history of cod fishing in 7 buildings. Halfway, don't miss Hamnøy: the red cabins seen from the bridge are perhaps the most famous photo of the Lofoten.

Kvalvika beach is one of the most beautiful in the Lofoten: a cove of white sand encircled by 400-meter peaks plunging into the Arctic. It's accessed on foot from the Fredvang parking lot, 1 hour walk each way on a marked trail. Bring waterproof shoes. Along the way, the Fredvang bridges - two suspension bridges over a strait between three islands - are one of the most sought-after photo spots in the Lofoten.

In late afternoon, make the detour to Nusfjord, one of the best-preserved fishing villages in the Lofoten, nestled at the end of a fjord. Entry is paid but gives access to a village that seems straight out of the 1900s. Take the path that climbs at the entrance for a panoramic view before descending to walk on the dock.

activities.text: The Reinebringen hike (448 m) is the most famous in the Lofoten. The ascent is on a staircase of 1,600 stone steps laid in 2019 by Nepalese workers accustomed to Himalayan trails. Allow 1h30 to 2h climbing. At the summit, the plunging view over the Reinefjord, Kjerkfjord and Vorfjord with the red cabins below is the most photographed panorama in the Lofoten. Leave early - the parking lot fills up quickly in season.

Spend the morning at Rambergstranda or Skagsanden beach, two white sand beaches easily accessible from the road. In good weather, the reflection of the mountains in the water is striking. The water is cold but some swim in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands
Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: Welcome to the Lofoten. The Narvik-Svolvær route via the E10 (3-4h) is an experience in itself: it runs along fjords, passes through tunnels carved into rock and crosses suspension bridges between islands. Svolvær, the de facto capital of Lofoten with 5,000 inhabitants, lives to the rhythm of its harbor. Head down to the quays: fishing boats, cod drying racks, rorbuer on stilts reflecting in the water. Skrei fishing (Arctic cod) remains the archipelago's engine - between January and April, Svolvær sees the world's largest cod catches.

Look up from the harbor: the Svolværgeita dominates the city from the flank of Mount Fløyfjellet. These two 150-meter rock pinnacles, nicknamed 'the Svolvær Goat', are the city's emblem. Climbers from around the world come to scale them. For the view without ropes, climb to Fløya (590 m, 2h from the center) - guaranteed 360° panorama over the archipelago.

Push open the door of the Gunnar Berg Gallery, dedicated to the painter born here in 1863. His canvases capture the light of the Lofoten in the 19th century - stormy skies, reflections on water, fishermen's silhouettes. A way to understand these landscapes differently than through photos.

activities.text: Take a speedboat excursion to Trollfjord, a 2 km fjord arm squeezed between vertical granite walls - so narrow that large boats can barely maneuver there. This is also where white-tailed eagles, with their wingspan of over 2 meters, dive to catch fish. Operators offer this outing departing from the harbor.

days_plan.itin_title: Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: Bodø→Tromsø flight (~1h). Tromsø: an island connected to the mainland by a one-kilometer bridge, surrounded by fjords and mountains 350 km north of the Arctic Circle. With 77,000 inhabitants, it's Norway's eighth largest city, nicknamed the 'Paris of the North' - a nickname from the 19th century, when upper-class women had their dresses imported directly from Paris and nightlife was surprisingly lively for such a northern city. Start with the Arctic Cathedral (Ishavskatedralen), built in 1964-1965 by architect Jan Inge Hovig: its triangular silhouette symbolizes icebergs, the long darkness and the northern lights. The eastern stained glass window - 140 m² of glass - is one of Europe's largest.

The Polar Museum (Polarmuseet) occupies a former 1833 customs warehouse, classified as national heritage since 2000. It traces whaling, sealing, and the great polar expeditions. Roald Amundsen - first man to the South Pole in 1911 - has an entire room, as does Fridtjof Nansen. Allow 1h30.

In late afternoon, stroll through Tromsø's old town: the colorful wooden houses from the 19th and 20th centuries are among the best preserved in Northern Norway. The wooden church in the center (Tromsø Domkirke, 1861) is Norway's only Lutheran wooden cathedral.

Start with the Fjellheisen cable car, in service since 1961. In four minutes, it takes you up to 420 meters on Mount Storsteinen. The panorama embraces the city on its island, the one-kilometer bridge, the fjords and mountains on the horizon. In winter, it's one of Tromsø's best spots to observe the auroras. In summer, it's the starting point for hikers heading to Tromsdalstinden (1,238 m).

Stop by Mack Brewery, founded in 1877 by Ludwig Markus Mack, son of a German immigrant. Mack long remained the world's northernmost brewery. Its historic pub, Ølhallen, open since 1928, is Tromsø's oldest brewery-bar. It's the perfect spot for an Arctic beer facing the old docks.
A detour through Polaria, the Arctic aquarium whose building evokes white concrete dominoes toppled by a wave. Inside, bearded seals, Arctic fish and an exhibition on phenomena of the Far North.

activities.text: In the dark season (September-March), a nighttime northern lights excursion from Tromsø is essential. Operators offer outings by minibus or boat to the surrounding fjords. Tromsø is one of Europe's best bases for this: the sky is statistically clearer more often than at equivalent latitudes.

If you have a car, head out to Kvaløya island or the Lyngen Alps for wild fjords less than an hour away. Local hikers go there year-round. In the evening, prepare for the return flight to Stockholm the next day.

days_plan.itin_title: Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands
Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: Arrival at Abisko Östra around 11am after 17 hours by train. Abisko National Park, created in 1909, covers 77 km² of birch valleys, Arctic mountains and tundra along Lake Torneträsk. Start at the Naturum Abisko visitor center to orient your stay and pick up a trail map.

In the afternoon, follow the Abiskojåkka canyon trail - allow 1 hour round trip for the short version, longer if you also climb upstream below the railway bridge. The river has carved a deep gorge into the rock. Tip: head up toward the mountains from Abisko Turiststation, not just toward the lake - the most impressive viewpoints are on this side.

Late in the day, descend to the shore of Lake Torneträsk, Scandinavia's largest mountain lake with its 330 km² of glacial water framed by peaks. On clear days, you'll spot the famous U-shaped Lapporten pass (Čuonjávággi in Sami), a glacial valley carved by glaciers that remains one of Lapland's iconic symbols. The wood-fired sauna right on the shore can be reserved at Abisko Turiststation the same morning.

Full day in the park. From Abisko Turiststation, you're at the official starting point of the Kungsleden - the King's Trail - 440 kilometers of trail all the way to Hemavan. You don't have to hike the whole thing: the first stage to Lake Abiskojaure (14 km one way) already offers a full day of hiking through birch forests, riverbanks and Arctic meadows. Bring waterproof shoes - the trails cross several streams.

In summer, guided hikes to the summit of Nuolja (1,164 m) depart each evening from the Aurora Sky Station to enjoy the midnight sun - the guide offers sparkling cider at the summit with views over the Abisko Alps, Torneträsk and Lapporten.

activities.text: If you're here from September to March, the Aurora Sky Station on Mount Nuolja is the evening rendezvous. The chairlift - Sweden's longest - takes you up to 900 meters altitude. At the top, a panoramic terrace and heated café. Abisko benefits from an exceptional microclimate: the mountains create a 'blue hole' that regularly clears the sky even when it's raining in Kiruna 100 km away. Book ahead, spaces are limited.

If you prefer to stay in the valley, the easy trail along both banks of the Abiskojokk to the Torneträsk shore (3.5 km round trip, accessible to all) is perfect for observing Arctic birds: Arctic loon, ptarmigan, northern warbler. The park is also home to moose and lynx, discreet but present.

days_plan.itin_title: Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands
Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: Åre is Sweden's premier mountain resort – and one of Scandinavia's most important. In winter, it's the country's most popular alpine domain, host of the Alpine Ski World Championships (1954, 2007, 2019). In summer, the crowds vanish and the village returns to human scale: a few lively streets, restaurants, a central square where hikers and mountain bikers mingle.

The Kabinbanan cable car climbs in 7 minutes from the central square (421 m) to the upper station at 1274 m – 853 m elevation gain in cabins accommodating 75 people. From the station, an additional 30-minute walk leads to Åreskutan summit (1420 m) and Toppstugan – Sweden's highest café, operating since 1892, where they serve waffles without running water or electricity (everything is carried up by hand). The panorama from up there over Jämtland's ranges is the perfect endpoint for this circuit.

The village of Fröå Gruva, east of Åreskutan, marks the site of an old mine surrounded by hiking and cross-country ski trails. A bit off the main tourist flow, it's a quiet way to close out the day before catching the train.

activities.text: Åre is renowned for downhill mountain biking – the lifts operate in summer to bring riders up. Outfitters offer rentals and downhill courses. If you're not a mountain biker, the marked hiking trails around the massif allow 5-15 km loops with beautiful views over Lake Åresjön below.

Åre is connected by train to Östersund (1h) and Stockholm (6h30), as well as to Storlien and Norway to the west (30 min to the border). If you continue to Norway, the train journey through the Jämtland valley is one of the country's most beautiful – plan to sit window-side.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: Borgholm Castle is an imposing ruin atop a rocky plateau overlooking the sea. Begun in the 16th century and expanded until the 18th, it burned in 1806 and was never rebuilt. Its ochre sandstone walls and gutted towers have a romantic allure. The interior is open to visitors, and summer concerts are held here.

Solliden Royal Palace, the Swedish royal family's summer residence since 1906, is partially open on the garden side. The private apartments remain closed, but the formal French-style parterres and flowered terraces are worth visiting.

The Stora Alvaret is a limestone steppe extending across 260 km² in the island's center and south. This almost lunar landscape—low grasses, exposed bedrock, wild orchids in spring—is one of Europe's rare examples of this ecosystem. It earned Öland UNESCO World Heritage status in 2000.

Ottenby bird reserve, at the island's southern tip, is one of Scandinavia's most important migration sites. Over 300 species have been recorded here. At the cape's extremity, Långe Jan—"Tall John"—is Sweden's tallest lighthouse at 41 meters.
Öland has several hundred windmills, about forty still standing along the east coast. The most accessible cluster is near Lerkaka, with five mills side by side in a meadow.

activities.text: Öland is perfectly flat—ideal cycling territory. Rental shops in Borgholm offer bikes for touring southward. The coastal cycle path follows the east shore for dozens of kilometers with views toward the mainland.
Birdwatching at dawn in Ottenby: in spring and autumn, migrations of cranes, geese, and raptors are spectacular from sunrise onward.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands

visits.text: Funäsdalen is the life centre of mountainous Härjedalen: a mountain village at the foot of Funäsdalsberget, surrounded by over 30 peaks exceeding 1000 m in the area. The Härjedalens Fjällmuseum, inaugurated in 1999, is one of Sweden's most modern museums. It presents, in beautiful exhibitions, the region's history through mountain farmers, Sami people and craftspeople – collections among the country's oldest. It received a special mention at the 2001 European Museum of the Year Awards.

Push on to Tännäs, about twenty kilometres east, to see the musk oxen at Myskoxcentrum. In 1971, five musk oxen spontaneously crossed the Norwegian border and settled in the mountains west of Tännäs – forming the basis of the current herd. The centre, created in 2010, offers guided visits at a respectful distance. These animals, covered in their thick woolly coats, look like something out of prehistory – because that's exactly what they are. Book ahead.

activities.text: Funäsdalsberget (the relief just above the village) offers a half-day hike accessible to all. From the summit, the view carries to Sylarna in the north and Sonfjället in the south – a panorama embracing 30 peaks. In summer, the ski runs transform into marked trails.

Nordic Ski Center (NSC) at Funäsfjällen claims one of the world's longest groomed cross-country ski networks, with 300 km of trails. In summer, these same trails become mountain bike and hiking paths. Outfitters offer mountain bike rentals on-site.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: The STF Grövelsjön Fjällstation stands at 816 m altitude on the flank of Långfjället, at the northern tip of Dalarna, right against the Norwegian border. It's one of the STF (Swedish Tourist Association) mountain stations – lodging, restaurant and shop included. Settle in: you're at the gateway to exceptional and uncrowded hiking territory.

Lake Grövelsjön itself stretches like a banana for 15 km, straddling the Swedish-Norwegian border – its northern end lies in Norway. From Sjöstugan, 1.5 km from the fjällstation, a boat crosses to Sylen on the Norwegian shore (crossing about 40 min). You'll pass into Norway without noticing – the border runs right through the middle of the lake.

The Linnaeus Trail (Linnéstigen) starts from Sjöstugan and climbs toward Norway following the exact route taken by botanist Carl von Linné in 1734 during his journey through Dalarna. On Långfjället, a sign marks the spot where Linnaeus observed falconers capturing wild falcons for hunting training – a remnant of a falconers' camp whose ruins still survive.

activities.text: The hike to the summit of Storvätteshågna (1204 m) is the day's major excursion – the highest point in Svealand, with a dizzying panorama over Norwegian fells, Töfsingdalen and the Helags massif to the north. Count on 24 km round trip from the fjällstation, roughly 7-8 hours. If you leave at 8am, you have plenty of time. The final ascent from Fosksjöarna is the steepest section.

For a more relaxed day, take the morning boat from Sjöstugan to Sylen (Norway), then walk back via the Linnaeus Trail (9 km return along the lake, easy terrain). You "enter" Norway without a passport, no customs, nothing – just a name change on the map. Silverfallet, a pretty waterfall 7 km from the fjällstation, is an even shorter alternative.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: Idre is a small village set close to the Norwegian border in northwestern Dalarna. The surrounding region belongs to the fjäll – those bare high plateaus above the treeline characteristic of Scandinavia. Take time to drive slowly as you arrive: the landscape shifts gradually from dense forest to bare, windswept ridges.

The Städjan-Nipfjället nature reserve deserves the detour. Nipfjället rises to 1192 m and the 16 km² reserve was created in 1974 (expanded in 2010) to protect unique Arctic flora and fauna: reindeer, foxes, numerous mountain raptors. Neighbouring Städjan (1131 m) is the southernmost summit of the Swedish mountain massif – its characteristic volcano-like profile stands out from afar.

activities.text: The loop hike up Städjan (6 km, ~380 m elevation gain) from the Nipstugan car park is the region's flagship excursion. The 360° panorama from the summit embraces Norwegian fells to the west and Dalarna's forests to the east. Count on 2.5-3 hours. The trail is well marked but the terrain is rocky on the upper section – proper footwear essential.

Shorter alternative: the ascent to Lillnipen (1100 m), starting from the upper Nipfjället car park, 1.5 km round trip. Very easy, perfect if you just want to feel the fjäll wind without exertion. The view over the Härjedalen fells to the east and Fulufjället to the west is well worth the minimal effort.

Idre Fjäll, the ski resort a few kilometres north of the village, offers 25 marked mountain bike trails in summer including the famous Städjan DH (rated black). Outfitters rent equipment on-site. It's one of Sweden's best-known MTB parks.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: Kalmar Castle is one of Sweden's best-preserved Renaissance castles. Here in 1397 the Kalmar Union was signed, a treaty uniting Sweden, Denmark, and Norway under a single crown—a union that lasted until 1523. The castle was remodeled in the 16th century by the Vasa kings, and its restored interior halls rank among Scandinavia's finest.
Kalmar's baroque cathedral dates from the 17th century—built to plans by Nicodemus Tessin the Elder, the royal court architect. Its yellow facade and dome are visible from across the lower town.

Old Kalmar, rebuilt after a 17th-century fire, follows a regular grid plan with low wooden houses painted red, ochre, and yellow. Walk the Kalmarsund waterfront to see traditional boats moored facing Öland island.

activities.text: In the afternoon, cross the Öland Bridge—6 km of concrete over the sea, toll-free, opened in 1972 and still Sweden's longest bridge. The drive across with views of the Kalmarsund on a clear day is a journey highlight in itself. You can stop on Öland for an initial reconnaissance before returning to sleep in Kalmar.
Kalmar harbor fisheries still serve fresh Baltic catch—perch, pike, and smoked eel are the local essentials.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands

visits.text: Karlskrona was founded in 1680 by King Charles XI as the main naval base for Sweden's Baltic fleet—the entire city was planned around its shipyards. It's the world's only still-operational baroque naval base, earning UNESCO World Heritage status in 1998. Start at the grand Stortorget, one of Sweden's largest baroque squares, flanked by two twin circular churches.

The Maritime Museum (Marinmuseum) traces 350 years of Swedish naval history with models, cannons, uniforms, and a section on Baltic battles. Outside, you can board the HMS Neptun submarine, a real Cold War-era submarine open for tours—the passageways are narrow, the atmosphere claustrophobic, and it's fascinating.

The Admiralty Church (Amiralitetskyrkan), built in 1685, is the largest surviving wooden church in Sweden. Before its entrance stands a wooden statue of an old lighthouse keeper who has been begging since the 18th century—tradition calls for dropping a coin in his bowl.
Karlskrona's archipelago extends across dozens of islands and islets around the city. Local ferries reach the main inhabited islands from the central harbor—some shelter 18th-century fortifications.

activities.text: Rent a kayak from the harbor to paddle along the fortifications from water level—the view of the city and bastions from sea level differs dramatically from the quayside perspective.
Karlskrona's covered market (Saluhallen) offers smoked Baltic fish, Swedish cheeses, and Scanian gingerbread—a good option for a quick lunch.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands

places.name: Ljungdalen

visits.text: The Flatruet road stops you dead the moment you emerge from the forest: you're at 975 m altitude, on Sweden's highest road, inaugurated in 1938. Before you, a bare, windswept plateau without trees – and reindeer on the tarmac, indifferent to your car. Stop at the ridgeline car park: in clear weather, you'll spot Helagsfjället and its glacier to the north. The road is partly gravel, closed between October and May.

Ljungdalen is a tiny village at the foot of Helagsfjället. It's the starting point for reaching STF Helags Fjällstation, 12 km hiking from the Kläppen car park (by car from Ljungdalen, 6 km). The mountain station is the base for the glacier ascent.

Helagsfjället (1797 m) is Sweden's highest peak south of the Arctic Circle – and on its flanks lies Helags glacier, Sweden's southernmost glacier. The summit is visible from afar: it dominates the surrounding topography spectacularly. National Geographic ranked it among the world's three best "toppturer" (summit climbs).

activities.text: The ascent of Helagsfjället from the fjällstation is 8 km round trip (or 24 km from Kläppen car park). From the 1797 m summit, the view carries over Sylarna to the north, Norway to the west and the Härjedalen fells in all directions. On the climb, you skirt the southern edge of the glacier – a geological curiosity in the heart of Scandinavia. Count on 4-5 hours from the fjällstation. Bring warm clothing even in summer: weather changes very quickly here.

If you don't want to climb to the summit, the hike from Kläppen to the fjällstation (12 km one-way, Kesudalen valley and birch forest) is already beautiful. You can sleep at the fjällstation and return the next day. Book ahead: limited spaces.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

map_keywords: Flatruet route haute Suède Mittådalen Ljungdalen

visits.text: Begin with the Västra Hamnen district, the former industrial port redeveloped into a model residential quarter in the 2000s. Here rises the Turning Torso: 190 meters of twisted white concrete designed by Santiago Calatrava and completed in 2005. It remains Scandinavia's tallest residential tower.

Stortorget, Malmö's main square, is framed by the 16th-century city hall and the governor's residence. Head down to Lilla Torg, the cobbled small square lined with half-timbered houses where locals gather in the evenings—it's one of the city's liveliest spots.

Malmöhus Castle dates from 1434—it's the oldest surviving Renaissance castle in Scandinavia. Today it houses several museums: natural history, art, and a section devoted to the city's history. Allow at least 90 minutes.

The medieval old town, centered around Stortorget and Sankt Petri Cathedral (14th century), deserves an hour of wandering. Behind its austere facade, the church conceals a 16th-century Flemish altarpiece and a burial crypt.

activities.text: Late in the day, head to the Ribersborg baths: a long wooden pier extending into the sea with gender-separated bathing cabins dating from 1898. Even out of season, regulars plunge into the cold waters of the Øresund.
Walk through Möllevångstorget, the working-class southern district's market, to see Malmö away from tourist circuits: vegetable stalls, spice aromas, neighborhood cafés.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands

visits.text: Lake Siljan spreads before you like an inland sea – 350 km² formed 377 million years ago by a meteorite impact, making it one of Europe's largest impact craters. Its shores are lined with wooden houses painted in dark red (the famous Falu rödfärg, a paint made from copper pigments extracted from the Falun mine) – the most quintessentially Swedish tableau imaginable.

The Zornmuseet holds the world's largest collection of works by Anders Zorn (1860-1920), a Mora-born painter who created portraits of three American presidents – Grover Cleveland, William Taft and Theodore Roosevelt. Don't miss Zorngården, his house preserved exactly as it was when his wife Emma died in 1942: guided tours in English run at 3pm Tuesday through Sunday. Allow 1.5 hours for both.

In Nusnäs, a few kilometres from Mora, you'll find the workshops where the famous Dalecarlian horses (Dalahäst) are hand-carved and painted – Sweden's unofficial symbol for generations. The craftspeople work before your eyes; it's far more engaging than any museum.

On Mora's main square stands the Vasaloppet finish gate – the line that 15,000 cross-country skiers reach every first Sunday in March after 90 km from Sälen. A statue commemorates Gustav Vasa, whose ski escape in 1520 inspired the race. In 1521, the people of Mora had initially refused to join him, before changing their minds and catching up with him in Sälen to launch the uprising against the Danes.

activities.text: Cycle around Lake Siljan (Siljansleden, roughly 310 km for the full circuit – take a section) or rent a kayak to paddle its calm waters. In summer, several beaches offer swimming: Siljansbadet in Rättvik, 30 km away, is one of the finest. Local outfitters provide rentals.

If you're here in mid-June, don't miss Midsommar: Dalarna is the beating heart of Sweden's Midsummer celebration. In Leksand, on the lakeshore, over 30,000 people gather around the maypole each year – one of the country's largest celebrations.

In the evening, stroll through central Mora – barely 10,000 inhabitants, yet packed with cafés, restaurants and craft shops. It's also the starting point of the Inlandsbanan, the legendary train that runs 1,300 km through Sweden all the way to Lapland.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: Östersund is the only real city on the entire circuit – 60,000 inhabitants, capital of Jämtland, founded in 1786 by King Gustav III on the shores of Storsjön. It's a jolt of modernity after the fells: cafés, restaurants, street life. Take an hour to stroll along the lake before visiting.

Jamtli is Jämtland's regional museum, 1 km north of the centre. It combines an indoor museum with an exceptional collection – including the Överhogdal tapestries, discovered 50 km north of Sveg, parts of which date back to the 9th century and constitute Europe's best-preserved Viking tapestries – and an open-air museum with historic 18th-19th century buildings where costumed actors have recreated peasant life every summer since 1986. The Storsjöodjuret exhibition notably presents the giant trap built in 1894 to capture the beast, baited with a live pig.

The island of Frösön, linked to Östersund by a bridge, is Jämtland's historic heart, predating the city by centuries. It holds Sweden's northernmost runestone, erected between 1050 and 1080 – and the monster carved on the stone is supposedly the Storsjöodjuret. Legend says a wizard named Ketil Runske imprisoned the beast in the stone by spell. Frösön church (late 12th century) has a separate bell tower typical of Jämtland – the view over Östersund and the lake from the island is the region's finest.

Along the shores of Storsjön, Storsjöodjuret statues punctuate a pleasant walk. The creature was first mentioned in writing in 1635. In 1986, for Östersund's bicentenary, it was even placed under legal protection – a law repealed in 2005. There are now 200 documented testimonies from people claiming to have seen it.

activities.text: In summer, Storsjön lends itself to kayaking and stand-up paddling – outfitters offer rentals and outings from Badhusparken. In calm weather, paddling on the lake while watching Östersund's silhouette reflected in the water has something quite surreal about it.

Östersund has been a UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy since 2010 – remarkable for a city this size. In the evening, try the downtown restaurants showcasing Jämtland products: game, mountain cheeses, wild herbs. It's the circuit's only real "city night".

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: At Berga By, a small, unassuming sign marks the official starting line of the Vasaloppet – the 90 km that have connected Sälen to Mora since 1922. The inaugural race on 22 March 1922 brought together 119 skiers starting from here; the course record now stands at 3h28. Setting foot on this starting line gives concrete scale to what the skiers are about to traverse.

The Högfjällshotellet, built in 1937, is the oldest mountain hotel in the region – and one of Sweden's most beloved. Even if you're not staying here, the architecture and the view from its terrace over the snow-covered (or summer-green) slopes merit a stop.

activities.text: In summer, the Vasaloppsleden transforms into a hiking trail over the historic 90 km route between Sälen and Mora, marked by eight refuges. You don't have to do it all: choose a 10-20 km section from Sälen toward Smågan, the race's first checkpoint. The trail crosses pine forests, gentle hills and frozen lakes – the same landscape that 15,000 skiers traverse every March.

Sälen is also Sweden's largest alpine ski domain, with several resorts clustered around the massif. In summer, the pistes convert to mountain bike trails and outfitters offer bike rentals and guided descents. The region is far less crowded than in winter – take advantage.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: Welcome to Gamla Stan, the birthplace of Stockholm: the old town spreads across three islands - Stadsholmen, Riddarholmen and Helgeandsholmen - between Lake Mälaren and the Baltic Sea, exactly as it was in the 13th century when Birger Jarl founded the city here in 1252. The ochre and red facades dominating Stortorget, Stockholm's oldest square, once belonged to a medieval fortress. This same square was also the site of the 1520 Stockholm Bloodbath, one of Scandinavia's most infamous political massacres. Arrive early in the morning before the tour groups - the silence of the cobbled streets at dawn is worth as much as the monuments themselves.

At the heart of Gamla Stan, the Royal Palace (Kungliga Slottet) contains over 600 rooms and remains one of the official residences of the Swedish monarch. Built in the 17th and 18th centuries in baroque style, it houses several museums including the Royal Treasury and the Tre Kronor Museum. If you're here in summer, don't miss the changing of the guard - it takes place daily at 12:15pm and is accompanied in July-August by the military mounted band.

Get lost in Mårten Trotzigs Gränd, Stockholm's narrowest alley at just 90 centimeters at its tightest point, then stroll along Österlånggatan to wander past the Hanseatic houses that recall how German merchants controlled much of Baltic trade from these walls in the Middle Ages. In the evening, Stortorget becomes one of Stockholm's best spots to dine on a terrace facing the colorful facades.

Dedicate your morning to the Vasa Museum on the island of Djurgården. The Vasa was the most impressive warship of its era: 64 cannons, 700 sculptures, 1,200 tons. It sank on August 10, 1628, twenty minutes after leaving Stockholm harbor, after sailing just 1,300 meters. Salvaged 98% intact in 1961 after 333 years on the harbor floor, it's now the only 17th-century vessel preserved in the world. Allow at least 2 hours and arrive at opening (8:30am in summer) to see the ship without the tour group crowds.

After the Vasa, stay on Djurgården for Skansen, the world's largest open-air museum: 160 historic buildings dismantled throughout Sweden and reconstructed here, forming a miniature 19th-century Sweden. It's also a zoo with moose, lynx and reindeer. If the weather cooperates, the island deserves a long walk along the quays.

In late afternoon, head to Södermalm, Stockholm's bohemian district south of Gamla Stan. Climb to Monteliusvägen for the best panoramic view of the old town at sunset - locals gather here in the evening for exactly this spectacle. The neighborhood is filled with vintage shops, galleries and restaurants.

Tromsø→Stockholm flight (~2h30). Back in the Swedish capital with a fresh perspective after 13 days in the Arctic. Make the most of these final hours with a canal cruise from Nybrokajen or Slussen - the best way to understand that Stockholm is built on 14 islands.

The City Hall (Stadshuset, 1923) on Kungsholmen island hosts the Nobel Prize banquet every December 10 since 1930 - in the Blue Hall, which isn't blue: architect Ragnar Östberg changed his mind at the last minute and kept the red bricks exposed, but the name was already printed on the plans. The guided tour tells this anecdote.
Djurgården island just off central Stockholm
ABBA The Museum on Djurgården
Vasa Museum with preserved 17th-century warship
Ethnographic Museum
Skansen open-air museum and zoo
Gröna Lund amusement park
Djurgårdsbrunn Canal for scenic walks

activities.text: Settle in and enjoy the evening for dinner in Gamla Stan or Södermalm - this is your first night in Scandinavia, take your time. Tomorrow you'll have a full day in Stockholm before boarding the overnight train.

To understand why Stockholm is built on 14 islands, take a ferry from Nybroviken to Vaxholm (45 min) or the archipelago islands. In summer, some Stockholmers swim directly from the rocks. It's the best way to grasp that the city and water are one.

Tonight, board the SJ Nattåg 94 overnight train from Stockholm Central. Departs around 6pm, arrives at Abisko Östra the next day around 11am - 17 hours of travel crossing Sweden from south to north. Book in advance: seats go fast in aurora season and summer. You can choose between a seat in an open car, a 6-berth sleeper compartment, or a private 2-3 person cabin. This train is a journey in itself - the landscapes between Kiruna and Abisko in the last hour are among the most beautiful of the crossing.

Last evening in Stockholm: climb to Monteliusvägen or Fjällgatan in Södermalm for one last panoramic view of the old town lights at twilight - the view that sums up Stockholm better than any postcard.
Departure from Stockholm. Arlanda airport is 40 minutes from the center by Arlanda Express from Stockholm Central. Plan to arrive 2h to 2h30 before departure.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands
The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands
The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands
Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands
Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands
Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands
Scandinavia Road Trip: Stockholm to the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: You'll arrive in Sveg through seemingly endless forests – Härjedalen is Sweden's least populated province, and you feel it. At the main crossroads, you'll run straight into it: the giant wooden bear standing 13 m tall, weighing 80 tonnes, built between 2003 and 2005 on 570,000 nails. It has become the symbol of the town and the entire province. The brown bear is Härjedalen's emblem animal – and the province holds one of Sweden's highest brown bear densities.

Sveg's Gammelgården is an open-air museum run by the Härjedalen handicraft association, founded in 1913. It gathers historic buildings from the region and traces local culture. At Överhogdal, 50 km north of Sveg, lie Europe's oldest Viking tapestries – the Överhogdal tapestries, parts of which date back to the 9th century – visible at Jamtli in Östersund.

Sveg's Inlandsbanan station is a stop on the legendary line that connects Kristinehamn to Gällivare over 1,300 km through central Sweden. The Inlandsbanan only runs in summer (June-August), one train daily in each direction. If you're passing in July, go watch the train depart.

activities.text: 80 km north of Sveg, Sonfjället National Park merits an excursion. Its volcano-like silhouette is visible from afar. It holds one of Sweden's densest brown bear populations – guides offer bear safaris at sunrise and sunset. Tracks are frequent even without spotting the animal. The summit Lilltoppen (approx. 1000 m) is accessible without major difficulties from the north entrance.

Alternatively, Björnberget, the local mountain 8 km from central Sveg, offers a pleasant half-day walk with views over Härjedalen's forests and hills. In the evening, Sveg is the sleepy provincial capital – 2,500 inhabitants – but the atmosphere is authentic, with no tourist artifice.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Central Sweden: Dalarna, Härjedalen & Jämtland

visits.text: This morning, cross back over the Öland Bridge to the mainland, then drive north to Oskarshamn (~80 km from the bridge, about 1 hour). This is where ferries depart for Gotland. Board with Destination Gotland, crossing takes about 3 hours—book ahead in July-August as spaces sell out. Arriving in Visby by ferry from Oskarshamn, the first thing you'll see entering the harbor: the ramparts. These 3.4 km of medieval walls, built between the 12th and 14th centuries, rank among Northern Europe's best-preserved. UNESCO-listed since 1995, they feature 44 towers and have never been demolished. Walk the complete circuit—allow 90 minutes.

Visby's ruined medieval churches are among the town's most distinctive features. Gotland once had 16 churches for a population of just a few thousand in the Middle Ages—most were abandoned after 1361 when Danish King Valdemar Atterdag captured the city. The ruins of St. Catherine's (Franciscan, 13th century) and St. Nicholas are the most photogenic.

Fiskargränd, the "Fisherman's Lane," is covered with blooming roses from June to August. It's one of Gotland's most photographed streets—narrow, cobbled, with low houses whose gardens overflow onto the lane.

Gotland Museum (Gotlands Museum) holds the world's largest collection of painted rune stones—these slabs illustrated with mythological scenes date from the 5th to 11th centuries. The treasury section displays thousands of Viking-era coins found in the island's fields.

Take the ferry to Fårö, the small island north of Gotland (free crossing, 5 minutes). This is where Ingmar Bergman lived and worked from 1967 until his death in 2007—he filmed several movies here including Persona and Scenes from a Marriage. The house is visible from outside; a center devoted to his work screens films in summer.

Fårö's raukar are limestone pillars sculpted by marine erosion over thousands of years. These strange 3-6 meter formations rise from beaches and moorlands like petrified figures. The most accessible are at Langhammars and Digerhuvud.

Gotland has over 90 rural medieval churches, some dating to the 11th century. They look similar from outside—limestone, square tower, interior paintings—but each conceals different details. The churches at Gothem, Garde, and Roma are among the most remarkable and freely open to visitors.

activities.text: In the evening, Visby comes alive around Stortorget and the old town lanes. Restaurant terraces set up in medieval house courtyards—it's an atmosphere quite different from mainland Sweden.

If you're arriving in summer (late July - early August), check dates for Visby Medieval Week (Medeltidsveckan): one of Europe's largest medieval festivals, with costumes, jousting, and markets throughout the old town.

Rent a bicycle on Fårö to link wild beaches and raukar—the island is small (about 30 km long) and lightly trafficked. Sudersand beach is the finest, with white sand and water that can reach 20°C in July.
Back in Visby in the evening, climb the northern ramparts to watch the sun descend over the Baltic Sea—in summer, light lingers until 10 PM.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands
The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands

visits.text: Between Malmö and Ystad, detour to Smygehuk: Sweden's southernmost point, marked by a small red-and-white striped lighthouse on a rocky coast. Nothing spectacular—but it's the geographic extremity of the country.

Ystad is a well-preserved medieval town with cobbled lanes, tightly packed half-timbered houses, and a 13th-century Franciscan monastery, Klostret, now converted into a regional museum. Fans of the Wallander crime series will recognize the settings—exteriors were filmed here.

Don't miss Ales Stenar at Kåseberga, 10 km east of Ystad. This megalithic monument of 59 standing stones forms a 67-meter ship-shaped ellipse on a grassy clifftop facing the Baltic Sea. Built around 600 AD, its exact purpose remains debated—solar observatory, burial site, or commemorative monument. The sea view at sunset is among the finest in Scania.

activities.text: Drive down to the fishing village of Kåseberga below the cliff to see boats pulled up on the shore and artisanal eel smokehouses—smoked eel is a local specialty.
In the evening at Ystad, walk along Stora Östergatan, the best-preserved medieval street, and look for a café in one of the hidden courtyards behind the facades.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand South: From Malmö to Stockholm via the Islands
